# Questions in Livestream 37

Live Stream #37: Normal Temp Before Mummification

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=260" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:20</a> | When Jonah fled from the presence of the Lord, was he specifically fleeing the temple? | Partly in a metaphorical sense, but Falk said Jonah's real motive was refusing the mission to Nineveh, not merely leaving temple space. | Falk said the temple language works because ancient localism could picture the Lord's presence as located at the temple, so Jonah is in that sense turning his face away from it. But he grounded the main point in Jonah 1: Jonah has been told to go to Nineveh, hates that mission, and heads toward Tarshish in the opposite direction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=485" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:05</a> | How should the word "satan" be understood in Numbers 22? | Here it means an adversary rather than the later proper name for the devil. | Falk read the Balaam passage as using "satan" functionally: the angel of the Lord stands in Balaam's way as an opponent or adversary. He distinguished that usage from treating the term as the proper name of a particular evil spiritual being, noting that the word can mean adversary, opponent, or accuser depending on context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=616" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:16</a> | Did Pharaoh Necho really receive a message from God, and should Josiah have listened? | Falk said the text reports Necho's claim, not an independent confirmation, though Josiah probably should have avoided the fight on political grounds. | Falk emphasized that the wording comes from Necho's testimony and may reflect how Necho described the situation rather than an independent narrator's confirmation that God directly spoke to him. Even so, he said Josiah should have consulted the Urim and Thummim before war, because kings were supposed to seek the Lord; Josiah was playing regional politics by blocking Necho's move toward Carchemish and may not have listened even if warned. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=904" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:04</a> | If European scholarship is moving away from the classic documentary hypothesis, is it moving toward unity or even further away? | He said the field is now more fragmented, with the supplementary hypothesis having more traction than a return to simple unified authorship. | Falk said the answer depends on which scholars are in view. Some European scholars favor a supplementary model in which a core Torah document receives additions over time, and he treated that as in some ways further from unified authorship than the classic documentary hypothesis. Others allow for a much older core that could move closer to unity, but his overall assessment was that the old consensus has fractured and the supplementary hypothesis currently has significant traction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=1128" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">18:48</a> | Are the Megiddo stables really Solomonic, and what evidence is for or against that? | No. Falk said the identification came from forcing unrelated texts together, while the ceramic evidence dates the remains much later. | Falk argued that early excavators joined 1 Kings 9:15, where Solomon refurbished Megiddo, to 1 Kings 10:26, where Solomon had horses and stables, and then labeled the Megiddo stables Solomonic. He said ceramic typology instead places the remains in the eighth century under Jeroboam II, when Israel was providing horses and chariots in an Assyrian satellite economy. The issue is not that the stables merely look late, but that the ceramics date them well after Solomon. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=1425" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:45</a> | Did Ezekiel 29's scattering of Egyptians happen under the Persians? | He said the best fit is earlier Assyrian deportation policy, though later invasions could also recontextualize the prophecy. | Falk rejected a Persian-period setting for Ezekiel himself and treated the prophecy as much earlier than the Persian era. He said prophecy can be recontextualized in later generations, but the scattering language fits Assyrian practice especially well because Assyrian conquests routinely deported and relocated peoples to prevent revolt, including in the northern kingdom and in Egypt. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=1545" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:45</a> | What is his opinion of Maggie Bryson's views on the Exodus? | He respected her as an Egyptologist but said her Exodus comments were not well informed. | Falk said he had met Bryson and considered her a nice person and a capable Egyptologist, but he thought her biblical use of the Exodus was sloppy. As an example, he said her point that there is no evidence of Israelites worshiping Yahweh in Egypt is technically true, but the biblical account itself would not expect that before the exodus and before Moses brings Yahweh's name back from Midian. His criticism was aimed at her Exodus comments, not at her Egyptological credentials. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=1712" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:32</a> | Did Moses go to Sheol, and was his shade brought out at the transfiguration? | Yes to both, in Falk's view; he treated Moses as among the righteous dead before the resurrection. | Falk said that before the resurrection, the spirits of the dead went to Sheol, which he described as having a good side for the righteous and a bad side for the wicked. Moses would have been on the righteous side, unlike Elijah and Enoch, who did not die in the same way. He also said Moses' shade could appear at the transfiguration, comparing the general idea of a dead person's spirit appearing to Samuel in the Endor episode. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=1918" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:58</a> | How many Egyptian kings can be identified from the Bible? | He said several can be identified directly or indirectly, including Shishak and Hophra, with a few others appearing by toponym or title. | Falk listed direct and indirect identifications such as Shishak/Sheshonq I, Osorkon IV, Taharqa, Necho II, Hophra, Ramesses II by the city-name Ramesses, Merneptah through toponyms, and Augustus Caesar as a pharaoh in Egyptian monumental usage. He did not present the count as exhaustive, but said the number is substantial enough to show that identifiable Egyptian rulers do appear in biblical contexts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=2063" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:23</a> | What are his favorite bands? | He said his tastes are broad and shaped partly by early work in music retail, rather than by one narrow genre. | Falk described eclectic musical tastes, mentioning Japanese and anime music, Band-Maid, Wagakki Band, trance music such as Tangerine Dream, Lacuna Coil, Metallica, and soundtracks such as Nausicaa, Vampire Princess Miyu, and Blade Runner. He said working in a department-store music section after college exposed him to many albums and helped broaden those tastes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=2204" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">36:44</a> | Is archaeology partly fueled by the same kind of fantasy represented by River Song and Doctor Who? | Yes, in the harmless sense that archaeologists do dream about big finds, and he added that Doctor Who helped inspire his own interest in archaeology. | Falk said archaeologists do fantasize about the big dig or big find, though most would rather avoid the dangerous adventure tropes. He specifically credited Doctor Who, especially the Tom Baker episode "Pyramids of Mars," with inspiring his interest in Egyptology, while clarifying that River Song herself was not the inspiration. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=2382" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">39:42</a> | Are there extra-biblical sources for King Og, and how big does the Bible imply he was? | Falk said he was not aware of extra-biblical sources for Og and did not want to claim more than the evidence allowed. | Falk said he did not know of extra-biblical sources for Og offhand and would need to look further before making a claim. He kept the answer limited rather than speculating, saying his lack of awareness may simply mean he does not know the relevant inscriptions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=2446" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">40:46</a> | Would we expect Joseph to appear under his Egyptian name, and are there sites where his name might still be found? | If found at all, it would likely be under his Egyptian name and most plausibly at Avaris, though Falk said the preservation there is so poor that he doubts it will happen. | Falk said Joseph's Egyptian name has an Egyptian etymology, so any archaeological identification would likely use that form. He pointed to Avaris as the plausible location because of Joseph's setting, but said preservation there is very poor: excavation lies below the water line, salt-laden water destroys papyrus and stone, and even the Hyksos palace scriptorium preserved only tiny tablet fragments and clay seals. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=2715" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:15</a> | Is there extra-biblical evidence for Nebuchadnezzar's madness in Daniel 4? | No known external evidence, and Falk said such a record would be unlikely because ancient regimes would avoid recording that kind of royal disgrace. | Falk said he was not aware of any extra-biblical evidence describing Nebuchadnezzar's madness. He noted that there is evidence for later Babylonian figures such as Belshazzar and for a rapid succession of kings after Nebuchadnezzar, but not for the madness itself. He also doubted that such a humiliating royal condition would normally be recorded, since writing about a king's madness could be treated as dangerous or treasonous. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=2870" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:50</a> | Could Seti I's official Mahi have been Moses, later erased by Ramesses II? | No. Falk rejected the identification on both chronological and etymological grounds. | Falk said Moses likely fled long before Seti I, probably during Horemheb's reign, because Horemheb's strict law-and-order regime would fit the aftermath of Moses killing the taskmaster. He also suggested the "daughter of a king" language may be retrojected after Ramesses I comes to the throne. On top of the chronological problem, he said the proposed connection between Mahi and Moses was not etymologically good. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=3068" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">51:08</a> | Should a student already taking German start Hebrew now or wait for college? | He said starting Hebrew alongside advanced German is workable, especially if the student is already past the beginner stage in the first language. | Falk said a student who already has the basics of German can begin Hebrew, especially if German is at a third-year or refinement stage rather than another first-year language. He noted that graduate students often juggle multiple languages, and Hebrew is not an exceptionally heavy language to start. For ancient Near Eastern study, he said German, French, Hebrew, and other ancient languages will all become useful. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=3261" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:21</a> | When did the divine name Yahweh become too holy to pronounce, and would the prophets have said it aloud? | He placed the shift around the second century BCE and said earlier prophets would have pronounced Yahweh normally. | Falk said the substitution of Yahweh with titles such as Adonai begins to appear around the second century BCE, including in some textual traditions that replace the Tetragrammaton with Lord-language. He described later Jewish practice as further elevating substitute terms, eventually using Hashem and Masoretic vowel pointing to cue readers to say Adonai. In the prophetic period, however, he said the name would have been pronounced as Yahweh. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=3479" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:59</a> | What should we make of claims that Semitic religion was basically Saturn or astrology worship? | He dismissed that as an oversimplification built on conjecture; astral associations exist, but Semitic religion is much more complex than that. | Falk acknowledged a limited truth behind some astral associations, such as Ishtar being associated with Venus, but said that does not reduce Semitic religion to astrology or Saturn worship. He pointed to other divine personifications such as sea, death, lordship, and peace to show that many forces contributed to the religion. His response was that online versions of the theory flatten a much more complex religious development. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=4081" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:01</a> | How old does he think Moses was during the Exodus? | Falk estimated roughly forty to fifty, treating the three forties in Moses' life as idiomatic long periods rather than exact chronology. | Falk said the age of eighty depends on treating the three periods of forty years as literal, but he takes forty as an idiom for an indeterminate long period. He estimated Moses fled Egypt in his early twenties, returned about thirty years later, and was therefore likely in his forties or fifties. He also thought the picture of Moses lifting his wife and children onto a donkey fits a younger man better than an eighty-year-old. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=4252" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:10:52</a> | Is there a Mesopotamian king or dynasty that matches Nimrod's accomplishments in Genesis 10:10-12? | He said possible candidates such as Sargon of Akkad or Naram-Sin have been suggested, but he had not studied the issue enough to give a firm answer. | Falk read the Genesis description of Nimrod's kingdom and said he did not yet have a settled candidate. He noted that some have suggested Naram-Sin or Sargon of Akkad, and that there were several relevant dynasties around Nineveh, but he had not done enough study to endorse one identification. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=4483" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:43</a> | How should someone memorize material and focus on studies with exams a few months away? | He advised sleeping enough, reviewing early and often, avoiding last-minute cramming, and focusing on steady work rather than panic over grades. | Falk said sleep is one of the biggest aids to memory because it helps information move into long-term memory. He recommended repeated review from the beginning of a course, saying familiarity often takes many passes through the material and creates a structure for later lessons. He warned that last-minute cramming can be counterproductive and told the student to use the remaining months by studying early, often, and steadily rather than spiraling over grades. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=4919" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:59</a> | Could the Ramesses II scarab from the Mount Ebal altar challenge both an early Exodus date and the documentary hypothesis? | He said it creates problems for both early-date advocates and critical scholars, since the find points toward a late-thirteenth-century setting that neither side easily absorbs. | Falk said the Ramesses II scarab and related Mount Ebal material are awkward for early-date advocates because the evidence points to a late-thirteenth-century setting, and attempts to preserve an earlier Israelite altar looked ad hoc to him. He also said critical scholars dislike the data because even a Ramesside setting is too early for many documentary-hypothesis timelines. He compared it to the Megiddo stables: archaeological data can force a correction of interpretation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=5367" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:27</a> | Is there a cultural reason Genesis and Exodus name Mesopotamian and Canaanite kings but leave Egyptian pharaohs unnamed? | Yes. Falk said New Kingdom Egyptian texts often call the king simply "Pharaoh," and that usage fits the cultural period reflected in the Exodus story. | Falk said Egyptian texts of the eighteenth and nineteenth dynasties often refer to the king simply as Pharaoh, though they could also give the king's full names. He treated that practice as a useful cultural and chronological marker, beginning around the reign of Thutmose III and continuing through the New Kingdom period. In his view, Israelites coming out of Egypt shared that cultural habit of seeing Pharaoh as an institution as much as an individual. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=5576" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:32:56</a> | Does Terah being from Urkesh invalidate the use of Ur Kasdim in Genesis? | No. He argued Ur Kasdim may distinguish this Ur from Sumerian Ur and may even preserve a form related to Urkesh. | Falk said Ur Kasdim can distinguish the relevant Ur from Ur of Sumer, which he argued is geographically problematic because it is outside Mesopotamia proper and does not fit the travel route as cleanly. He also suggested the wording may preserve or reflect Urkesh, with added letters making the place name intelligible to a later audience. He treated "of the Chaldeans" as explanatory rather than as a reason to abandon Urkesh. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=5835" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:37:15</a> | Could river course changes explain Ur of Sumer being in Mesopotamia? | Falk doubted it, because a river shift large enough to solve the problem would likely have destroyed the site. | Falk allowed that rivers can change course, but he said this explanation creates a bigger problem for Ur of Sumer. If the river had moved far enough to put the site on the needed side, the river's erosion would likely have wiped out or obliterated the site, so he did not think a course-change solution was reasonable. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=5884" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:38:04</a> | Why would Terah make such a big deal about moving only about twenty-five miles? | He said the importance was social and political, not just geographic: Terah would have moved from local power broker to displaced outsider. | Falk said the move was about life circumstances rather than mileage. He compared Terah of Urkesh to someone giving up rulership or local power, moving to north Syria, and then describing himself to the king of Mari as an Apiru or displaced outsider. The significant change was losing status and control, not simply traveling a short distance. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=6095" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:41:35</a> | Was Abraham royalty? | Falk said Terah looks more like a plutocrat or power broker than a royal-line figure, while Abraham still appears to have inherited substantial resources and retainers. | Falk said the Mari evidence makes Terah look powerful, perhaps a kingmaker or local power broker, but not clearly a member of a royal line. Abraham, however, appears to inherit significant resources after Terah's estate is divided, and his 314 trained retainers suggest a substantial household or private force for a sojourner in Canaan. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=6277" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:44:37</a> | Was Terah Nimrod's idol maker? | No. Falk said the chronology does not fit, since Nimrod would belong much earlier than Terah. | Falk said Terah dates to the eighteenth century BCE, while Nimrod would belong much earlier under the available conservative identifications. Because the timeframes do not overlap, he said Terah could not have been Nimrod's idol maker, even though the identity of Nimrod's idol maker is not otherwise known. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=6341" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:41</a> | If an Old Testament professor teaches a fifteenth-century BCE Exodus date, should a student correct it or let it be? | He advised the student to avoid unnecessary trouble in class, answer according to the professor's expected view when required, and keep studying the evidence independently. | Falk said the student should let it be, because challenging a professor on the Exodus date can create unnecessary academic trouble. He argued that many conservative institutions are tied to early-date expectations through churches, donors, and institutional pressures, and he described personal costs from holding a late-date view. His practical advice was to answer according to the professor's view for class purposes while continuing to study the evidence privately. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=6592" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:52</a> | How do we know the Merneptah Stele is not referring to Israel suffering famine or defeat by Canaanites? | Falk said the poetic section reflects Merneptah's own campaign activity, with geography and comparanda such as Gezer supporting a direct encounter rather than famine. | Falk said the poetic section lists deeds by the king, moving through places and peoples connected to his own campaign. He pointed to a Merneptah scarab from Gezer as evidence that Merneptah reached that region, making an encounter with Israel plausible. He also said the stele's order reflects local geography and identifies Israel as a people group, so famine or defeat by Canaanites is not the best reading. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=6783" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:53:03</a> | If a primordial singular divine entity was inactive, why did Egyptians come up with an inert deity before active gods? | He explained it as an origin-of-the-gods problem: some Egyptian traditions began with primordial divine essence or waters from which active creator gods emerge. | Falk said the inert primordial reality answers, or partially answers, the question of where the gods came from. In Egyptian myth, primordial waters or divine essence can give rise to active creator gods such as Atum-Re, who then creates other gods and ordered features of the world. He contrasted this with Memphis theology, where Ptah is self-created, and said Egyptian traditions were creatively wrestling with divine origins without a developed idea of God as outside created time. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=7070" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:57:50</a> | When did coins come into Egypt, and what did Egyptians use before coinage? | Coins came in during the Greek/Ptolemaic period; before that, Egypt used barter systems valued by weights such as the bronze deben, along with grain measures. | Falk said coinage appears in Egypt in the Greek period, specifically under the Ptolemies, and named Ptolemy II for the earliest coinage he had in mind. Before that, Egypt functioned as a barter economy pegged to units of value such as the bronze deben, a weight of bronze rather than circulating money. He also mentioned other units, including gold deben, the seniu, and the khar as a sack of grain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=7175" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:59:35</a> | What is the Egyptian word for money? | Falk answered "deben," while noting it was a unit of value or weight rather than circulating coinage. | Falk had just explained that the deben was the value unit used in Egypt's barter economy before coinage. When asked for the Egyptian word for money, he answered "deben," but the surrounding answer makes clear that it functioned as a weight or accounting value, not as coins people carried around. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=7213" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:00:13</a> | Does he agree with Michael Heiser that Genesis 1 does not purport creation ex nihilo? | Yes. He said Genesis 1 presents fiat creation and a divine ordering of a pre-existing watery setting, not a direct claim of creation out of nothing. | Falk agreed that Genesis 1 does not directly teach creation ex nihilo, while still affirming fiat creation by divine command. He distinguished God saying "let there be" from creation out of nothing, noting examples like humanity being formed from soil. He read Genesis 1:1 possibly as a title, with verse 2 presenting a formless watery earth over which God's spirit moves, so the chapter describes God's ordering and forming work rather than an explicit account of absolute material origins. |
